data.content_ref:
inline:The target's mechanism of 'contagious rebellion' fails to account for the simultaneous destruction and abandonment of major administrative centers across culturally and linguistically distinct regions that lacked any unified subject identity or communication network capable of coordinating revolt.
The target claims localized revolts 'became contagious across the network,' but the interconnected palace civilizations of the Eastern Mediterranean did not share a common subject population. Mycenaean Linear B scribes, Hittite officials in Hattusa, and Ugaritic administrators operated in completely different languages, ethnic groups, and political hierarchies. There is no archaeological or textual evidence of a pan-regional subject class consciousness or communication channels that would allow revolts in Pylos to inspire copycat uprisings in Ugarit or Hattusa within the same generation. The near-simultaneity of destruction horizons (within decades) across these disconnected populations contradicts a mechanism requiring information contagion through a network of subjects who had no means or motive to coordinate across kingdoms.
